EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system centered near the eastern Great Lakes Region continued to strengthen as it moved northeast on December 23, 2022. This brought high winds over northeastern New Jersey that morning as the pressure gradient tightened over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a downed tree across Davis Avenue in Harrison, NJ at 502 AM  Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system moving through the area produced several rounds of heavy rainfall during the early morning of December 23rd. These rounds of heavy rainfall resulted in a widespread 1.5 to 3 inches of rainfall with localized areas of flash flooding and river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall allowed the Saddle River at Lodi to rise above minor flood stage of 5.5 ft at 8:15 AM on December 23rd. It kept rising to its crest of 6.27 ft at 12:45 PM. It then fell below minor flood stage at 3:15 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system centered near the eastern Great Lakes Region continued to strengthen as it moved northeast on December 23, 2022. This brought high winds over northeastern New Jersey that morning as the pressure gradient tightened over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NJ Department of Highways reported a tree down on Route 208 south of Cedar Hill Drive in Wyckoff, NJ at 356 AM Local Standard Time. This closed the right lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex weather system brought precipitation to the mid-Atlantic on March 12. Strengthening low pressure tracked from the Southeast US to just off the mid-Atlantic coast, and began to rapidly intensify as it departed towards New England. Unseasonably warm air ahead of the system and its close proximity to the coast caused precipitation to start as rain. However, as the low deepened and as winds shifted to a northwest direction, colder air began to invade, and rain gradually changed to snow from west to east. In some areas, precipitation remained steady to heavy for a few hours after the changeover, allowing several inches of snow to accumulate. Dropping temperatures also led to instances of flash freezing. With the storm rapidly deepening as it moved by and away from the area, winds also increased, exacerbating impacts caused by the wet snow and leading to scattered power outages. More tranquil conditions returned to the region by March 13.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 3 to 6 inches of snow fell, with a trained spotter south of High Point reporting 5.3 inches. Wind gusts over 60 mph were recorded near High Point along with scattered power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front approached the mid-Atlantic late on March 7. An unusually warm air mass overspread the region ahead of it, with several climate sites experiencing record high temperatures well into the 70s on the 7th. As the front approached during the evening, a line of low topped convection developed ahead of it. While instability was limited, the very strong wind fields and frontal lift allowed this line to produce widespread wind gusts of 50 to 65 mph as it moved through. This resulted in scattered to numerous reports of downed trees and power outages. The front moved offshore by late in the evening with calmer and more seasonable weather returning for the following day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on US-202 in both directions north of Sunnybranch Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak wave of low pressure passing offshore brought light precipitation to the mid-Atlantic on March 9. With marginal temperatures, much of the precipitation fell as rain or a mix of rain and wet snow. However, colder air and a locally heavier band of precipitation in more northern areas such as the Poconos and northern New Jersey allowed for a few inches of snow accumulation to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 2 to 4 inches of snow fell, with a maximum report of 4.3 inches received from High Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather system brought steady to heavy precipitation to most of the mid-Atlantic on March 23rd and 24th. This fell mainly as rain, but some icing occurred in the highest elevations of the Pennsylvania Poconos and far northern New Jersey. Impacts were mostly negligible.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icing occurred in the highest elevations of northern Sussex County. A report of 0.30 inches of ice accretion was received from High Point, with ice mainly accumulating on elevated and non-paved surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of low pressure passed southeast of the mid-Atlantic early on February 7. Some light precipitation was spread back into the region, mainly falling in the form of light freezing rain and drizzle. Areas of light ice accretion were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain and freezing drizzle fell. A trained spotter in Hamilton Square reported 0.01 inches of ice accretion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training clusters of thunderstorms developed near the intersection of the sea breeze and an outflow boundary approaching from the south. This resulted in an area of heavy rainfall across northern Ocean, southern Monmouth, and eastern Burlington Counties which produced some locally significant flash flooding. Some isolated wind damage was also reported in northern Ocean County. Another cluster of heavy thunderstorms developed late in the evening in Mercer County and across the river in Bucks County, PA and tracked northeast producing locally heavy rainfall as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS station NJ-OC-71 5.6 miles northeast of Manchester Twp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure remained off the Middle Atlantic Coast from October 2nd through 5th, before moving out to sea on the 6th.  The low maintained an onshore flow along the coasts of New Jersey and Delaware during that period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread roadway flooding and some property inundation occurred in the tidal areas of Ocean County.  The tide gauge at Waretown reached 3.37 feet MLLW and the tide gauge at Tuckerton reached 5.55 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong coastal storm affected the eastern mid-Atlantic and Northeast US on January 28-29, 2022. As a deep trough moved into the Southeast US, low pressure began to develop off the coast of the Carolinas during the afternoon of January 28. A high amplitude upper level steering pattern caused the developing low to begin moving north-northeastward, paralleling the US East Coast. In a classic Nor���easter evolution, the developing low phased with a frontal system over the mid-Atlantic as it moved north. This frontal system had brought light snow to portions of the mid-Atlantic during the day on the 28th, and it also brought a fresh influx of cold air to the region. As it phased with the developing coastal storm, the coastal storm began to explosively intensify as it passed offshore of Delmarva. The strengthening storm spread precipitation back into the region, which fell as all snow thanks to the fresh injection of Arctic air. With the center of the low passing a few hundred miles offshore, the heaviest snow fell near the coast, from the night of the 28th through the morning of the 29th. In eastern New Jersey and coastal Delaware, several hours of heavy snow resulted in a widespread swath of 12 to 18 inches of snow. Amounts steadily tapered off heading further inland, though some snow fell in all of the local area. In addition, with the rapid strengthening of the low, strong winds also occurred, especially near the coast. Gusts of 40 to 50 mph with a few over 60 mph were observed. The combination of strong winds and heavy snow led to whiteout conditions along the coast, and was sufficient for blizzard criteria to be met along both the New Jersey coast and the Delaware Beaches, making this the first blizzard to affect any portion of the region since 2018. Snow gradually lost intensity and came to an end during the afternoon of the 29th, as the still strengthening low continued moving northward, bringing blizzard conditions and even heavier snow to southeast New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread 8 to 14 inches of snow fell. A COOP observer in Margate City reported 11.1 inches of snow. Wind data from coastal mesonets and visibility data from the KACY ASOS were used to confirm blizzard conditions occurred from around 4AM to 11AM on the 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing wave of low pressure brought light precipitation to the mid-Atlantic on the morning of January 5, 2022. Warm air advection caused rising temperatures aloft over the region. However, surface temperatures were initially sub-freezing, and in some cases took several hours after precipitation began to move above freezing. This caused freezing rain to fall. A widespread light icing event transpired across the eastern mid-Atlantic as a result of this. While ice accumulations were no more than several hundredths of an inch, this event caused significant travel impacts during the busy morning commute hours of January 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain fell. The Millville Airport (KMIV) ASOS reported 0.03 inches of ice accretion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm affected the mid-Atlantic and much of the Eastern Seaboard from January 16-17, 2022. A strong negative tilt trough over the Southeast US spurred strong cyclogenesis near the Carolina coast late on January 16. This low would proceed to quickly deepen as it moved northward that evening and night. Upper level steering patterns caused the low to take an ���inside runner��� track which kept the low center inland of the coast. A very strong easterly low level jet developed in between the strengthening low and a departing high pressure center to the north. With the center of the low tracking west of the region and an influx of marine air courtesy of the low level jet, various precipitation types occurred during this event. An Arctic air mass had been in place in advance of this system, but due to the aforementioned factors, that air mass rapidly modified on the 16th. As precipitation arrived, temperatures remained cold enough for it to begin as snow in most areas away from the coast. However, a rapid transition from snow to mixed precipitation to rain occurred in most areas within hours. Frozen precipitation held on for longer across the interior, where some higher snowfall amounts occurred. The storm departed the region by early on January 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report via social media with a video.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deepening low pressure passed off the Middle Atlantic Coast on January 3.  The resulting onshore flow caused moderate coastal flooding in the tidal areas of Cape May County and Atlantic County around the time of the morning high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate tidal flooding occurred and there were numerous road closures.  The tide gauge at Sea Isle City reached 6.71 feet MLLW, the tide gauge at Stone Harbor reached 7.10 feet MLLW, the tide gauge at Cape May Harbor reached 7.45 feet MLLW, and the tide gauge at the Cape May Ferry Terminal reached 7.98 feet MLLW.

The tide gauge at Sea Isle City reached 6.71 feet MLLW, the tide gauge at Stone Harbor reached 7.10 feet MLLW, the tide gauge at Cape May Harbor reached 7.45 feet MLLW, and the tide gauge at the Cape May Ferry Terminal reached 7.98 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal low pressure along the polar front developed in response to an upper level disturbance. This result in an moderate snowfall across most portions of northeastern New Jersey, with some portions further east and south receiving heavier snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports of 5.0 to 7.3 inches were reported from the public, trained spotters, and CoCoRahs. 7.3 inches was reported in Closter, and 6.0 inches was reported in Lyndhurst, with a trained spotter reporting 5.6 inches in East Rutherford.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured 4.3 inches in Wayne.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amount of 4 to 5.4 inches were reported across Western Bergen County, with Mahwah reporting 4.0 inches and 2 miles NW of Upper Saddle River reporting 4.0 inches. Fair Lawn came in with 5.4 inches from a public report.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports right at or just above 6 inches was reported, with a public observation of 6.3 inches in West Orange, a trained spotter reporting 6.0 inches in North Caldwell, and a public report one west southwest of West Caldwell of 6.0 inches.

As it phased with the developing coastal storm, the coastal storm began to explosively intensify as it passed offshore of Delmarva. The strengthening storm spread precipitation back into the region, which fell as all snow thanks to the fresh injection of Arctic air. With the center of the low passing a few hundred miles offshore, the heaviest snow fell near the coast, from the night of the 28th through the morning of the 29th. In eastern New Jersey and coastal Delaware, several hours of heavy snow resulted in a widespread swath of 12 to 18 inches of snow. Amounts steadily tapered off heading further inland, though some snow fell in all of the local area. In addition, with the rapid strengthening of the low, strong winds also occurred, especially near the coast. Gusts of 40 to 50 mph with a few over 60 mph were observed. The combination of strong winds and heavy snow led to whiteout conditions along the coast, and was sufficient for blizzard criteria to be met along both the New Jersey coast and the Delaware Beaches, making this the first blizzard to affect any portion of the region since 2018. Snow gradually lost intensity and came to an end during the afternoon of the 29th, as the still strengthening low continued moving northward, bringing blizzard conditions and even heavier snow to southeast New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread 5 to 9 inches of snow fell. A trained spotter in Hopewell reported 9.2 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing wave of low pressure brought a period of snow to the eastern mid-Atlantic during the overnight of January 6 into the morning of January 7. The developing low was fast-moving, with steady precipitation lasting only 6 to 8 hours in many areas. However, the storm brought a widespread swath of 3 to 6 inches of snow to most of the eastern mid-Atlantic. Some portions of eastern New Jersey saw some slightly higher amounts of 6 to 7 inches, but in general it was a fairly uniform snowfall for most areas. With most of the accumulation occurring near or before dawn, roadway conditions were rendered poor for the Friday morning commute on the 7th, causing many businesses and schools to close or shift to virtual operations for the day. One notable aspect of this event was its higher than usual snow to liquid ratios for the mid-Atlantic. Temperatures were cold enough for precipitation to fall as all snow. With plenty of cold air at the surface and aloft, snow tended to be light and fluffy, causing it to pile up quickly despite liquid equivalents being a rather paltry 0.20 to 0.50 inches in most of the region. The ���snowfall ratio���, which often averages close to 10:1 or lower (i.e., 1 inch of liquid would equate to 10 inches of snow) was closer to 15:1 or even 20:1 in most reports containing both a snowfall and liquid equivalent measurement.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread 2 to 4 inches of snow fell. A trained spotter in Stockholm reported 4.0 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deepening low pressure passed off the Middle Atlantic Coast on January 3.  The resulting onshore flow caused moderate coastal flooding in the tidal areas of Cape May County and Atlantic County around the time of the morning high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate tidal flooding occurred and there were numerous road closures.  The tide gauge at the Atlantic City Marina reached 6.79 feet MLLW, and the tide gauge on the Atlantic City oceanfront reached 7.23 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal low pressure along the polar front developed in response to an upper level disturbance. This result in an moderate snowfall across most portions of northeastern New Jersey, with some portions further east and south receiving heavier snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At this point there are no official reports from Eastern Essex County. Right along the eastern border Newark Airport reported 5.2 inches, with likely estimates averaging below 6 inches across Eastern Essex.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Nor'easter tracked just east of the benchmark bringing snow and gusty winds. The highest snowfall totals occurred primarily across eastern sections. Wind gusts of 40 mph were reported across eastern parts of Northeastern New Jersey, with higher impacts to the east across New York City, Coastal SE New York, and Eastern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and blowing snow impacted Hudson County. Nearby, Newark, NJ reported wind gusts to 40 mph. A trained spotter reported 8.5 inches in Hoboken and the public reported 8.5 inches in Jersey City. A CO-OP observer reported 7.0 inches in Harrison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Nor'easter tracked just east of the benchmark bringing snow and gusty winds. The highest snowfall totals occurred primarily across eastern sections. Wind gusts of 40 mph were reported across eastern parts of Northeastern New Jersey, with higher impacts to the east across New York City, Coastal SE New York, and Eastern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The general public reported 7.1 inches in Newark. Other nearby reports were over 6 inches, in the neighborhood of 6 and 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Nor'easter tracked just east of the benchmark bringing snow and gusty winds. The highest snowfall totals occurred primarily across eastern sections. Wind gusts of 40 mph were reported across eastern parts of Northeastern New Jersey, with higher impacts to the east across New York City, Coastal SE New York, and Eastern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The general public reported 7.0 inches in West Orange, 5.2 inches in Livingston, and 5.0 inches in Essex Fells. Reports and surrounding reports suggest the eastern half of the zone had over 6 inches of snow, while the western half of the zone had closer to 5 inches. The combination of snow and blowing snow at times resulted in impactful winter weather.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm impacted most of Delmarva and southern New Jersey on January 3, 2022. A strengthening area of low pressure developed over the Southeast US late on January 2 and move northeast, tracking offshore of the mid-Atlantic in a typical Nor'easter-type setup. A widespread 6 to 12 inches of snow with locally higher amounts fell across the Eastern Shore of Maryland, most of Delaware, and several counties of southern New Jersey. The storm was notable for having a very sharp cutoff in the northern extent of accumulating snow. Also, an unseasonably warm air mass had been in place prior to this storm���s arrival, with areas that saw the most snowfall having been in the 50s and 60s less than 12 hours before snow began! The passage of a strong cold front brought rapid cooling in the hours leading up to the storm, and very heavy snowfall rates, at times well in excess of 1 inch per hour, overwhelmed the lingering warm ground and caused accumulation to occur. The storm was fairly quick moving, and had departed by the late afternoon and early evening of the 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across immediate coastal Cape May County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing wave of low pressure brought light precipitation to the mid-Atlantic on the morning of January 5, 2022. Warm air advection caused rising temperatures aloft over the region. However, surface temperatures were initially sub-freezing, and in some cases took several hours after precipitation began to move above freezing. This caused freezing rain to fall. A widespread light icing event transpired across the eastern mid-Atlantic as a result of this. While ice accumulations were no more than several hundredths of an inch, this event caused significant travel impacts during the busy morning commute hours of January 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain fell. A COOP observer near Milltown reported 0.02 inches of ice accretion. Numerous motor vehicle accidents occurred, reportedly several dozen in Middlesex County alone, including a fatal accident on Route 34 in Old Bridge.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 6.9 inches in Bergenfield and 6.2 inches in East Rutherford. A cocorahs report of 6.0 inches came in from North Arlington at 9:30 am before the snow had tapered off. The cold temperatures and gusty winds also resulted in blowing snow.

Wind gusts of 40 mph were reported across eastern parts of Northeastern New Jersey, with higher impacts to the east across New York City, Coastal SE New York, and Eastern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spottesr reported 5.0 inches in Westwood and 4.5 inches in Franklin Lakes, 4.0 inches in Lyndhurst, and 4.5 inches in Washington Township. The public reported 5.0 inches in Closter, 4.7 inches in Fair Lawn, 4.5 inches in River Vale, and 4.0 inches in Glen Rock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm impacted most of Delmarva and southern New Jersey on January 3, 2022. A strengthening area of low pressure developed over the Southeast US late on January 2 and move northeast, tracking offshore of the mid-Atlantic in a typical Nor'easter-type setup. A widespread 6 to 12 inches of snow with locally higher amounts fell across the Eastern Shore of Maryland, most of Delaware, and several counties of southern New Jersey. The storm was notable for having a very sharp cutoff in the northern extent of accumulating snow. Also, an unseasonably warm air mass had been in place prior to this storm���s arrival, with areas that saw the most snowfall having been in the 50s and 60s less than 12 hours before snow began! The passage of a strong cold front brought rapid cooling in the hours leading up to the storm, and very heavy snowfall rates, at times well in excess of 1 inch per hour, overwhelmed the lingering warm ground and caused accumulation to occur. The storm was fairly quick moving, and had departed by the late afternoon and early evening of the 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread 8 to 12 inches of snow fell in immediate coastal Atlantic County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing wave of low pressure brought a period of snow to the eastern mid-Atlantic during the overnight of January 6 into the morning of January 7. The developing low was fast-moving, with steady precipitation lasting only 6 to 8 hours in many areas. However, the storm brought a widespread swath of 3 to 6 inches of snow to most of the eastern mid-Atlantic. Some portions of eastern New Jersey saw some slightly higher amounts of 6 to 7 inches, but in general it was a fairly uniform snowfall for most areas. With most of the accumulation occurring near or before dawn, roadway conditions were rendered poor for the Friday morning commute on the 7th, causing many businesses and schools to close or shift to virtual operations for the day. One notable aspect of this event was its higher than usual snow to liquid ratios for the mid-Atlantic. Temperatures were cold enough for precipitation to fall as all snow. With plenty of cold air at the surface and aloft, snow tended to be light and fluffy, causing it to pile up quickly despite liquid equivalents being a rather paltry 0.20 to 0.50 inches in most of the region. The ���snowfall ratio���, which often averages close to 10:1 or lower (i.e., 1 inch of liquid would equate to 10 inches of snow) was closer to 15:1 or even 20:1 in most reports containing both a snowfall and liquid equivalent measurement.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread 4 to 5 inches of snow fell. A maximum report of 5.5 inches of snow was received from a COOP observer near Stanton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southwest flow aloft overran a retreating and shallow cold air mass along a weak stationary boundary. This resulted in a brief but impactful period of wintry mix of precipitation which included freezing rain during the early morning hours and into the initial stages of the morning commute, but most impacts occurred just before the start of the main part of the morning commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix quickly changed to freezing rain where .01 inches of ice accretion took place.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought locally heavy rain to Middlesex County on the night of January 1.  Rainfall amounts of 1.0 to 1.5 inches fell in a short amount of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in New Brunswick.  College Avenue was closed near NJ Route 18.  Joyce Kilmer Avenue was closed near Charles Street.  Jersey Avenue was under water near Tailer Square.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm affected the mid-Atlantic and much of the Eastern Seaboard from January 16-17, 2022. A strong negative tilt trough over the Southeast US spurred strong cyclogenesis near the Carolina coast late on January 16. This low would proceed to quickly deepen as it moved northward that evening and night. Upper level steering patterns caused the low to take an ���inside runner��� track which kept the low center inland of the coast. A very strong easterly low level jet developed in between the strengthening low and a departing high pressure center to the north. With the center of the low tracking west of the region and an influx of marine air courtesy of the low level jet, various precipitation types occurred during this event. An Arctic air mass had been in place in advance of this system, but due to the aforementioned factors, that air mass rapidly modified on the 16th. As precipitation arrived, temperatures remained cold enough for it to begin as snow in most areas away from the coast. However, a rapid transition from snow to mixed precipitation to rain occurred in most areas within hours. Frozen precipitation held on for longer across the interior, where some higher snowfall amounts occurred. The storm departed the region by early on January 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixed precipitation fell. After a brief period of snow, some freezing rain fell before precipitation changed to all rain. The Millville Municipal Airport (KMIV) ASOS reported 0.03 inches of ice accretion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing coastal low off the Mid-Atlantic resulted in strong winds along the coastal counties of NJ.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tuckerton Weatherflow site measured a 67 MPH gust.

The ���snowfall ratio���, which often averages close to 10:1 or lower (i.e., 1 inch of liquid would equate to 10 inches of snow) was closer to 15:1 or even 20:1 in most reports containing both a snowfall and liquid equivalent measurement.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread 2 to 4 inches of snow fell. A report of 3.0 inches of snow was received from Pennsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing wave of low pressure brought light precipitation to the mid-Atlantic on the morning of January 5, 2022. Warm air advection caused rising temperatures aloft over the region. However, surface temperatures were initially sub-freezing, and in some cases took several hours after precipitation began to move above freezing. This caused freezing rain to fall. A widespread light icing event transpired across the eastern mid-Atlantic as a result of this. While ice accumulations were no more than several hundredths of an inch, this event caused significant travel impacts during the busy morning commute hours of January 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Radar data combined with observations from surrounding areas confirm light freezing rain occurred, mainly over the eastern part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system impacted the mid-Atlantic on January 9, 2022. This was a relatively minor system, with a warm frontal passage in the morning followed by a cold frontal passage in the evening. As the warm front moved through, much warmer air moved in aloft. However, cold air lingered at the surface over portions of northern New Jersey and eastern Pennsylvania, as a cold and dry air mass had previously been over the region. This caused several hours of light freezing rain and drizzle to occur in some areas, with light ice accumulations reported. Precipitation gradually changed to plain rain, and/or came to an end entirely, as the day progressed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain fell. The Sussex Airport ASOS (KFWN) reported 0.03 inches of ice accretion.

A very strong easterly low level jet developed in between the strengthening low and a departing high pressure center to the north. With the center of the low tracking west of the region and an influx of marine air courtesy of the low level jet, various precipitation types occurred during this event. An Arctic air mass had been in place in advance of this system, but due to the aforementioned factors, that air mass rapidly modified on the 16th. As precipitation arrived, temperatures remained cold enough for it to begin as snow in most areas away from the coast. However, a rapid transition from snow to mixed precipitation to rain occurred in most areas within hours. Frozen precipitation held on for longer across the interior, where some higher snowfall amounts occurred. The storm departed the region by early on January 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous spotter reports of 3 to 5 inches of snow were received, with a maximum report of 5.5 inches in Green Pond. It is likely a light glaze of freezing rain also occurred before precipitation changed to rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong coastal storm affected the eastern mid-Atlantic and Northeast US on January 28-29, 2022. As a deep trough moved into the Southeast US, low pressure began to develop off the coast of the Carolinas during the afternoon of January 28. A high amplitude upper level steering pattern caused the developing low to begin moving north-northeastward, paralleling the US East Coast. In a classic Nor���easter evolution, the developing low phased with a frontal system over the mid-Atlantic as it moved north. This frontal system had brought light snow to portions of the mid-Atlantic during the day on the 28th, and it also brought a fresh influx of cold air to the region. As it phased with the developing coastal storm, the coastal storm began to explosively intensify as it passed offshore of Delmarva. The strengthening storm spread precipitation back into the region, which fell as all snow thanks to the fresh injection of Arctic air. With the center of the low passing a few hundred miles offshore, the heaviest snow fell near the coast, from the night of the 28th through the morning of the 29th. In eastern New Jersey and coastal Delaware, several hours of heavy snow resulted in a widespread swath of 12 to 18 inches of snow. Amounts steadily tapered off heading further inland, though some snow fell in all of the local area. In addition, with the rapid strengthening of the low, strong winds also occurred, especially near the coast. Gusts of 40 to 50 mph with a few over 60 mph were observed. The combination of strong winds and heavy snow led to whiteout conditions along the coast, and was sufficient for blizzard criteria to be met along both the New Jersey coast and the Delaware Beaches, making this the first blizzard to affect any portion of the region since 2018. Snow gradually lost intensity and came to an end during the afternoon of the 29th, as the still strengthening low continued moving northward, bringing blizzard conditions and even heavier snow to southeast New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread 12 to 18 inches of snow fell. A maximum total of 19.0 inches was reported by a trained spotter in Egg Harbor Twp. In addition, wind and visibility data from the KACY ASOS confirm that blizzard conditions occurred from about 4AM to 11AM on the 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing wave of low pressure brought light precipitation to the mid-Atlantic on the morning of January 5, 2022. Warm air advection caused rising temperatures aloft over the region. However, surface temperatures were initially sub-freezing, and in some cases took several hours after precipitation began to move above freezing. This caused freezing rain to fall. A widespread light icing event transpired across the eastern mid-Atlantic as a result of this. While ice accumulations were no more than several hundredths of an inch, this event caused significant travel impacts during the busy morning commute hours of January 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain fell. The Lakehurst Naval Air Station AWOS reported 0.06 inches of ice accretion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system impacted the mid-Atlantic on January 9, 2022. This was a relatively minor system, with a warm frontal passage in the morning followed by a cold frontal passage in the evening. As the warm front moved through, much warmer air moved in aloft. However, cold air lingered at the surface over portions of northern New Jersey and eastern Pennsylvania, as a cold and dry air mass had previously been over the region. This caused several hours of light freezing rain and drizzle to occur in some areas, with light ice accumulations reported. Precipitation gradually changed to plain rain, and/or came to an end entirely, as the day progressed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain fell. mPing reports and reports from surrounding areas suggest at least a light glaze of ice occurred in parts of the county.

With the center of the low tracking west of the region and an influx of marine air courtesy of the low level jet, various precipitation types occurred during this event. An Arctic air mass had been in place in advance of this system, but due to the aforementioned factors, that air mass rapidly modified on the 16th. As precipitation arrived, temperatures remained cold enough for it to begin as snow in most areas away from the coast. However, a rapid transition from snow to mixed precipitation to rain occurred in most areas within hours. Frozen precipitation held on for longer across the interior, where some higher snowfall amounts occurred. The storm departed the region by early on January 17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The pressure gradient tightened as a cold front approached from the west. This produced strong winds over Northeastern New Jersey early in the morning of February 18, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer reported a tree down on a car with 1 injury at 1232 AM local standard time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elongated frontal system brought a lengthy period of precipitation to the mid-Atlantic from late February 3 through February 4. A surge of warm air ahead of the front caused precipitation to fall as rain initially. As the front moved through, a wave of low pressure developed along it, prolonging precipitation as colder air drained into the region. This caused precipitation to change from rain to freezing rain, locally mixed with sleet or snow. A number of reports of light icing were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No direct reports were received, but reports from surrounding counties suggest at least trace amounts to a few hundredths of an inch of icing likely occurred in parts of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough approaches from the west along with associated weak surface low pressure. The surface low passed to the north and west, but dragged a warm front and cold front through northeast NJ. A round of severe thunderstorms moved through in the afternoon ahead of the cold front along a pre-frontal trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: House struck by lightning in Woodcliff Lake NJ.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak coastal low passed off the mid-Atlantic coast on February 13, 2022. This system brought widespread light snow to the region. A widespread 2 to 6 inches of snow fell over much of eastern Pennsylvania and New Jersey, with accumulations mainly occurring on cold and/or grassy surfaces due to antecedent warm conditions. Impacts were minimal overall. The fast-moving low departed out to sea by the afternoon of the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread 2 to 4 inches of snow fell. The highest amount received was from a trained spotter in Andover, who reported 4.4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked through western Pennsylvania on the night on February 24 into the morning of February 25. A weak secondary area of low pressure developed off the New Jersey coast on the 25th. This complex system brought a period of precipitation to the mid-Atlantic. With the main low tracking west of the region, warmer air rushed in aloft, limiting the potential for snow to fall. However, a strong Canadian high pressure to the north, combined with the developing secondary low, caused cold air to be trapped near the surface. This caused precipitation to fall as a wintry mix across the region, including as several hours of sleet and freezing rain. In some cases, the ice accretion was enough to cause tree and power line damage. The system pulled away from the region by the afternoon of the 25th with warmer and drier weather following behind it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixed precipitation fell. Snow and sleet accumulations of 1 to 2.5 inches were reported across the county, with some light freezing rain falling as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast along the Tennessee Valley and the Lower Appalachains and redeveloped along the Mid Atlantic coast as it moved northeast. A mix of sleet and snow changed to freezing rain, and eventually plain rain before ending. Snow amounts were fairly negligible where minor amounts of snow and sleet fell across northern areas before the change to freezing rain and plain rain. No impact LSRs were issued / reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Caldwell Airport ASOS reported 0.38 inches of elevated flat ice accretion (or 0.15 radial). West Caldwell via the public reported 0.30 inches of flat ice accretion, and Livingston via the public reported 0.15 inches of radial ice accretion measurement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the mid-Atlantic during the early morning hours of February 17, 2022. An unseasonably warm air mass was present ahead of it, and temperatures ahead of the front during the overnight hours remained in the 60s. The front was associated with very strong lift and wind fields. In fact, some locally damaging wind gusts occurred ahead of the front on the afternoon and overnight of the 16th. Along the front, a narrow, low topped line of showers and isolated thunderstorms developed. This line strengthened as it approached the Delaware Valley, and produced several instances of damaging wind near the I-95 corridor. The cold front and convective line would then sweep offshore soon after with seasonably cool weather following.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power outages were reported due to tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the mid-Atlantic during the early morning hours of February 17, 2022. An unseasonably warm air mass was present ahead of it, and temperatures ahead of the front during the overnight hours remained in the 60s. The front was associated with very strong lift and wind fields. In fact, some locally damaging wind gusts occurred ahead of the front on the afternoon and overnight of the 16th. Along the front, a narrow, low topped line of showers and isolated thunderstorms developed. This line strengthened as it approached the Delaware Valley, and produced several instances of damaging wind near the I-95 corridor. The cold front and convective line would then sweep offshore soon after with seasonably cool weather following.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large tree limbs were downed. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked through western Pennsylvania on the night on February 24 into the morning of February 25. A weak secondary area of low pressure developed off the New Jersey coast on the 25th. This complex system brought a period of precipitation to the mid-Atlantic. With the main low tracking west of the region, warmer air rushed in aloft, limiting the potential for snow to fall. However, a strong Canadian high pressure to the north, combined with the developing secondary low, caused cold air to be trapped near the surface. This caused precipitation to fall as a wintry mix across the region, including as several hours of sleet and freezing rain. In some cases, the ice accretion was enough to cause tree and power line damage. The system pulled away from the region by the afternoon of the 25th with warmer and drier weather following behind it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain fell before transitioning to plain rain. The South Jersey Regional Airport (KVAY) ASOS reported 0.19 inches of ice accretion, though this may not be representative of amounts as a whole, which were lower due to warm ground effects.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure developed along a frontal wave along the southeast coastline and moved northeast offshore as 700 mb frontogenesis contributed to a light accumulating snowfall with mainly low advisory and sub advisory level snowfall across Northeastern New Jersey. Not all zones are entered as with a warm ground some zones had under 2.5 inches of accumulated snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 3.1 inches at 2 NNE Franklin Lakes and the public reported 3.4 inches at 2 N Ramsey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast along the Tennessee Valley and the Lower Appalachains and redeveloped along the Mid Atlantic coast as it moved northeast. A mix of sleet and snow changed to freezing rain, and eventually plain rain before ending. Snow amounts were fairly negligible where minor amounts of snow and sleet fell across northern areas before the change to freezing rain and plain rain. No impact LSRs were issued / reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Orange via the public reported 0.25 inches of flat ice accretion. Nearby West Orange via the public reported 0.25 inches of flat ice accretion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passes to our north and west and dragged a cold front through northeastern NJ. Accompanying the cold front was a line of showers and thunderstorms, some of which produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS at Newark Airport measured a 57 KT wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm affected the mid-Atlantic and much of the Eastern Seaboard from January 16-17, 2022. A strong negative tilt trough over the Southeast US spurred strong cyclogenesis near the Carolina coast late on January 16. This low would proceed to quickly deepen as it moved northward that evening and night. Upper level steering patterns caused the low to take an ���inside runner��� track which kept the low center inland of the coast. A very strong easterly low level jet developed in between the strengthening low and a departing high pressure center to the north. With the center of the low tracking west of the region and an influx of marine air courtesy of the low level jet, various precipitation types occurred during this event. An Arctic air mass had been in place in advance of this system, but due to the aforementioned factors, that air mass rapidly modified on the 16th. As precipitation arrived, temperatures remained cold enough for it to begin as snow in most areas away from the coast. However, a rapid transition from snow to mixed precipitation to rain occurred in most areas within hours. Frozen precipitation held on for longer across the interior, where some higher snowfall amounts occurred. The storm departed the region by early on January 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant wind damage event occurred near and just north of Tuckerton NJ. Downed wires were reported on US-9 near Green St with all lanes closed. The most notable damage occurred a little further north, close to Freedom Fields County Park. Areal footage shot via a drone confirmed at least several dozen trees were snapped or uprooted in a wooden area. The pattern of damage was visually consistent with straight line wind damage. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms formed along a pre-frontal trough just ahead of the a cold front in PA and NY. The storms headed east and moved through northeastern NJ bringing damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Right lane of Garden State Parkway blocked with tree debris in Paramus NJ.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low over the Ohio Valley translated slowly east as a surface cold front moved toward the area. Ahead of the front a cluster of storms moved into the area from the southwest as a mid-level shortwave and pre-frontal trough provided lift.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on Graylock Parkway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A remnant mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) moved through the mid-Atlantic on the afternoon of May 20. This feature formed in association with a strong convective complex over the southern Plains on May 18, and proceeded to track northeast across the eastern half of the US, producing multiple additional rounds of severe weather as it did so. This included over the mid-Atlantic on May 20. As the MCV moved into the region, a warm and moderately unstable air mass was present over the region. Shear and forcing associated with the MCV caused multiple rounds of thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon. Some storms became severe, with a mixed convective mode leading to instances of damaging winds, hail, and one brief tornado in New Jersey. As the MCV moved offshore, convection diminished during the evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the mid-Atlantic late on May 16. Multiple areas of convection developed ahead of the front. With moderate to strong instability and shear, the environment ahead of the front appeared favorable for widespread severe weather. However, a messy convective evolution served to limit the overall threat. The greatest frontal forcing also ultimately tracked further north. However, some storms still became strong to severe and produced a few instances of damaging winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving, late season coastal storm affected the eastern mid-Atlantic from about May 7-9. The storm brought widespread rainfall, unseasonably cold temperatures, and strong winds especially near the coast. Many coastal areas experienced wind gusts over 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak gust measured by an NJ Weathernet site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms moved along a warm front of an approaching low pressure system on May 27th and into the 28th. These thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall over Northeast New Jersey resulting in several rounds of localized flash flooding. Several rounds of rainfall each with 0.75 to 1 inch of rain in an hour cause brief localized flash flooding. Total rainfall amounts in Northeast NJ were 1.5 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water rescue at the intersection of Johnston Avenue and Passaic Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three juvenile swimmers were caught in and rescued from a rip current near 10th St in Ocean City, NJ on the afternoon of May 21, with one serious injury. Weather conditions were hot and dry. Water conditions were near calm and the risk of rip currents was considered low. It was not clear whether the victims were swimming near a jetty or man-made structure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three juvenile swimmers were rescued by Ocean City Beach Patrol. One swimmer, a 12 year old, was seriously injured and had CPR performed before transfer to a trauma center. The other two were taken to the hospital as a precaution. Time of event based on media reports.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey has confirmed a brief EF-0 |tornado occurred in Hazlet Township on the evening of Friday, May |20th just after 5 PM EDT.||The tornado began along Line Road on the border between Strathmore |Township and Hazlet Township where several large limbs were blown |off and a couple softwood tree trunks were snapped. The snapped tree |trunks caused collateral damage to utility poles and power lines |near the intersection of Sophia Drive. The center of circulation |continued east-northeast into a residential neighborhood between |Sophia Drive and Carlow Way where the most significant damage from |the tornado occurred. At least a dozen homes in this area sustained |cosmetic damage, including vinyl siding blown off, soffit damage, |gutter damage, and/or roof shingles blown off. The only structural |damage noted was due to a tree falling onto a home which caused some |structural damage to its roof. A few larger softwood trees were |uprooted in the residential area and several vinyl fence sections |were blown over.||The tornado damage path then became somewhat discontinuous as it |crossed the Garden State Parkway toward another residential area |along Park View Drive. A softwood tree sustained significant loss of |limbs and a couple others were uprooted nearby. A utility pole was |also damaged along Park View Drive. A few homes sustained minor |cosmetic damage near the intersections of Linda Place, Beers Street, |and Rutgers Street, then the circulation continued east-northeast |along Rutgers Street toward Bedle Road where several large limbs |were blown off a few trees. A final softwood tree was uprooted along |Bedle Road near Indian Hill Court where the surface circulation |appeared to have dissipated.||No additional damage occurred from this storm as it continued |through the residential area toward Route 35. Four utility poles |were pushed over or broken along Route 35 near the Jiffy Lube auto |shop. However, no additional damage occurred in this area, and thus |it has been determined that the damage to the poles was caused by |straight line winds.

Shear and forcing associated with the MCV caused multiple rounds of thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon. Some storms became severe, with a mixed convective mode leading to instances of damaging winds, hail, and one brief tornado in New Jersey. As the MCV moved offshore, convection diminished during the evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a developing low pressure along a stationary front on July 16th. These thunderstorms quickly dropped 1-2 inches of rain in less than an hour resulting in localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on NJ 17 both directions north of NJ 120/Paterson Plank Road. Two out of three right lanes closed southbound. All northbound lanes closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the mid-Atlantic on April 14. Ahead of the front, unseasonably warm temperatures well into the 80s were present. While moisture was more limited, this still allowed for some weak to borderline moderate instability to develop. Combined with sufficient wind shear and forcing, scattered showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the front on the afternoon of the 14th. While atmospheric conditions caused the storms to mainly stay below severe limits, some locally strong to damaging wind gusts were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were downed along NJ-41 in Deptford Twp near Good Intent Rd. Power outages to homes, businesses, and traffic signals in the area were reported. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy thunderstorms developed in the warm sector of a frontal system on July 18th, 2022. These thunderstorms developed in an environment with 2 to 2.3 inch PWAT values and CAPE near 3000 J/kg. Training thunderstorms produced a widespread 1 to 3 inches of rainfall with localized spots up to 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All Northbound lanes closed due to flooding at Exit 69 - I-80 on the New Jersey Turnpike / I-95.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strengthening low pressure moved slowly up the Middle Atlantic Coast from the 18th into the 19th.  The resulting onshore flow caused moderate tidal flooding in parts of New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread roadway flooding occurred in the tidal areas of Middlesex County.  The tide gauge at Perth Amboy reached 8.43 feet MLLW.

The tide gauge on the Inside Thorofare in Atlantic City reached 7.05 feet MLLW.  The tide gauge at Margate reached 7.00 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the mid-Atlantic ahead of a cold front on April 16. The storms developed in an environment of little to no surface based instability. However, some elevated instability and cold air aloft, combined with strong frontal forcing, allowed storms to develop. Thanks to the steep lapse rates aloft, several storms produced hail. Hail was mainly sub-severe in nature, but a couple reports of hail close to one inch in diameter were received. Showers and storms moved off the coast by later in the evening of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An mPing report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms formed along a pre-frontal trough just ahead of the a cold front in PA and NY. The storms headed east and moved through northeastern NJ bringing damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large mature Oak tree uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inland tracking system brought warm advection precipitation to the region, with the first frozen precipitation of the season occurring in northern New Jersey and the southern Poconos.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow changed to sleet and freezing rain before a change to rain. A coating of snow around 1 occurred along with a glaze of ice up to one tenth of an inch. The Sussex ASOS (KFWN) reported 0.08 of ice accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through the area brought multiple rounds of rain on April 7th. The front approached and a low pressure system developed over the area resulting in moderate to heavy rain over Northeast New Jersey. 2 to 3 inches of rain fell over a several hour period. This resulted in river and poor drainage flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ramapo River at Mahwah rose above its minor flood stage of 8 feet at 11:30pm EDT. It then continued to rise before cresting at a height of 8.31 feet at 4:30am EDT on April 8th. The river then gradually receded, eventually falling below minor flood stage at 2:45pm EDT on April 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front, well ahead of associated surface and upper level low over Central Canada, moves through the area bringing with it a line of severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video shows significant damage to structure at Bergenfield High School in Bergenfield NJ. Bleachers, a field goal post and metal siding are seen strewn about the field. One minor injury was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A group of four swimmers was caught in a rip current off of a Wildwood, NJ beach late in the afternoon of May 31. Weather conditions were hot and dry, with relatively calm seas. The risk for rip currents was considered low. The beach was unguarded at the time. Following a 911 call, Wildwood firefighters rescued two swimmers, who had drifted 100 to 200 yards offshore. One reached shore on his own. The fourth, a 19 year old man, was not spotted after the initial distress call.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Time of event estimated from media reports. 19 Year old man died after he and 3 others encountered a rip current. Two others were rescued and one made it to the shore on his own.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system affected much of the mid-Atlantic on May 27. Following a warm frontal passage and ahead of a cold front, a weakly to moderately unstable and moderate to strongly sheared environment developed. As frontal forcing increased, scattered to widespread convective development occurred across the region, with messy, mixed mode storm structures dominating. While the messy storm mode and only somewhat favorable environmental conditions limited the coverage of severe weather, some storms did become strong to severe, producing instances of damaging wind. Convection gradually departed the region during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on North Main St at Lake Ave. Time estimated from radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Saddle River at Lodi rose above its minor flood stage of 5.5 feet at 9:15pm EDT. It then continued to rise, surpassing major flood stage (8.0 feet) at 2:45 am EDT on April 8th before cresting at a height of 8.99 feet at 7:00am EDT. The river then gradually receded, eventually falling below minor flood stage at 1:00pm EDT on April 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms that developed along a warm front on August 22nd produced heavy rainfall and localized flash flooding in New Jersey. The thunderstorms developed in an environment with PWATs increasing from 1.6 to 1.9 inches and CAPE values around 2000 J/kg. The storms quickly dropped 1 to 2 inches of rainfall in less than 90 minutes over portions of urban Northeast New Jersey that resulted in localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Person trapped in a car and water rescue is underway in Scotch Plains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed out ahead of an approaching cold front. Storm coverage and intensity increased as a potent shortwave lifted through the region. Severe storms around the Lehigh Valley and North Jersey formed in the vicinity of a warm front that was bisecting the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of penny to nickel size hail from the public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The pressure gradient tightened as a cold front approached from the west. This produced high winds over Southeastern New Jersey in the late afternoon to early evening hours of November 30, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 60 mph was recorded at a mesonet station located in Jersey City, NJ at 534 PM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training clusters of thunderstorms developed near the intersection of the sea breeze and an outflow boundary approaching from the south. This resulted in an area of heavy rainfall across northern Ocean, southern Monmouth, and eastern Burlington Counties which produced some locally significant flash flooding. Some isolated wind damage was also reported in northern Ocean County. Another cluster of heavy thunderstorms developed late in the evening in Mercer County and across the river in Bucks County, PA and tracked northeast producing locally heavy rainfall as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS station NJ-MC-49 1.7 miles southeast of Pennington.

Another cluster of heavy thunderstorms developed late in the evening in Mercer County and across the river in Bucks County, PA and tracked northeast producing locally heavy rainfall as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reports storm total rainfall of 4.14 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training clusters of thunderstorms developed near the intersection of the sea breeze and an outflow boundary approaching from the south. This resulted in an area of heavy rainfall across northern Ocean, southern Monmouth, and eastern Burlington Counties which produced some locally significant flash flooding. Some isolated wind damage was also reported in northern Ocean County. Another cluster of heavy thunderstorms developed late in the evening in Mercer County and across the river in Bucks County, PA and tracked northeast producing locally heavy rainfall as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reports 3.7 inches of rain measured in previous two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through late in the day, with showers and thunderstorms developing ahead of the front. A few thunderstorms that developed in New Jersey turned out to be severe, bringing damaging winds to portions of South Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of a large tree down in a backyard. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of onshore flow combined with 3-5 foot breaking waves resulted in dangerous rip currents developing over a 6 day stretch. Enhanced wave heights were due to Hurricane Earl passing well offshore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported a death off the coast of LBI near Loveladies where a man had died in the surf. The beach was unguarded, with a High Risk for rip currents was in effect.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of onshore flow combined with 3-5 foot breaking waves resulted in dangerous rip currents developing over a 6 day stretch. Enhanced wave heights were due to Hurricane Earl passing well offshore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Philadelphia media reported a fatality in Ocean City, NJ, likely caused by rip currents. Shortly after noon on Thursday, a man was pulled from the water unresponsive. The man was transported to the hospital, where he was pronounced dead. His son was injured trying to save him.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A batch of moderate rainfall with embedded heavy thunderstorms moved through the area on September 13th bringing flash flooding to the New York City metro area. These thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front with a low pressure developing overhead in an extremely moisture environment that had precipitable water values of 1.8-2. These thunderstorms dropped between 1 and 3 inches of rainfall in a several hour period resulting in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding on McClellan Street with traffic blocked.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moving through sparked a line of thunderstorms which trained over Northeast New Jersey late on September 25th and into the 26th. They developed only in a marginally moist environment with precipitable water values near 1.2-1.4 inches. These thunderstorms dropped 1-3 inches of rain over the area in a several hour period resulting in localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on US 22 Eastbound West of Fairway Drive. All lanes closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually long-lasting daylight dense fog event developed across most of New Jersey on December 31st, 2022. The event resulted due to much warmer moist air moving northward in association with a warm front, crossing over areas where the ground remained frozen following the severe cold snap a week earlier. The fog started developing across southeastern portions of the area, then spread northwestward, enveloping most of the area at various points during the morning, afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of dense fog was observed at Atlantic City International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northward from the eastern Carolinas on the 22nd to eastern Pennsylvania on the morning of the 23rd.  It was then absorbed by a stronger low moving across the eastern Great Lakes.  The system produced moderate to major tidal flooding in New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread roadway flooding occurred in the tidal communities of southeastern Burlington County.  The flood waters affected some vulnerable buildings.  The tide gauge at Tuckerton (Ocean County) reached 5.77 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front swept through the region with light precipitation lingering for a brief period in its wake. Temperatures plummeted below freezing before paved surfaces dried out. This resulted in icy areas on untreated surfaces.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lingering light precipitation in the wake of a strong Arctic cold front resulted in a flash freeze of untreated surfaces and icy roads as temperatures plummeted well below freezing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry system brought widespread precipitation to the region, including a coating of snow to portions of northern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread coating of 1 to 3 inches of snow fell across Sussex County. Isolated amounts up to 4 inches were reported in the higher elevations. The highest total was 3.8 inches in Montague Twp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deepening low pressure moved slowly northeastward from the Chesapeake Bay Region on the evening of December 15 to the waters off southeastern New England on the evening of December 16.  The system produced moderate tidal flooding in Ocean County, New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread roadway flooding occurred in the tidal communities of Ocean County, with the flood waters affecting some vulnerable buildings.  The tide gauge at Mantoloking reached 3.17 feet MLLW, the tide gauge at Bayshore reached 3.45 feet MLLW, the tide gauge at Waretown reached 3.75 feet MLLW, the tide gauge at Ship Bottom reached 4.10 feet MLLW, and the tide gauge at Tuckerton reached 5.57 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northward from the eastern Carolinas on the 22nd to eastern Pennsylvania on the morning of the 23rd.  It was then absorbed by a stronger low moving across the eastern Great Lakes.  The system produced moderate to major tidal flooding in New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread roadway flooding occurred in the tidal communities of Middlesex County.  Water entered vulnerable buildings causing some damage.  The tide gauge at Perth Amboy reached 9.39 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Medium to long period southeasterly swells resulted in an elevated risk of rip currents along the New Jersey coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 53 year old drowned while swimming at the beach at Wildwood Crest. This is suspected to be rip current related as the Police Chief described the surf as extremely rough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought locally heavy rain to parts of northern New Jersey on the evening of June 1.  Rainfall totals as high as 2 to 3 inches fell in a short amount of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred on several secondary roads in Mendham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought locally heavy rain to parts of northern New Jersey on the evening of June 1.  Rainfall totals as high as 2 to 3 inches fell in a short amount of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Route 22 was closed in both directions in North Plainfield due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A somewhat unusual convective environment developed on June 1. A wedge of warm, unstable air existed in between a backdoor cold front dropping from northeast to southwest and another cold front moving in from the west. This shrinking area of warm air was characterized by moderate to strong instability but only weak to moderate shear. A few areas of convection developed while conditions remained favorable, which produced localized instances of damaging winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed with a utility pole on fire on Fresh Ponds Rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the mid-Atlantic on June 2. A diffuse backdoor cold front that have moved into the area the prior day was also present near the Delmarva peninsula and southern New Jersey. A rather complex convective environment developed ahead of the approaching front, generally characterized by both moderate shear and instability. Widespread thunderstorm activity developed ahead of the front, with a mixed mode of multicells and a couple of supercells. Some of the storms became severe as they moved through the region, producing damaging winds and small hail. As the front moved offshore during the later evening hours, the severe weather threat diminished and a cooler, drier air mass settled in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was uprooted and another had large branches snapped off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front which moved through the mid-Atlantic during the mid afternoon hours of June 17. Hot temperatures were present ahead of the front, but shear and moisture were lacking, which kept thunderstorms isolated in nature. A couple of storms became strong to severe and produced locally damaging winds. Cooler, drier weather followed as the front swept offshore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local police department reported wires down. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A New Jersey Forest Fire Service spotter tower detected a wildfire in a remote section of the Wharton State Forest in Mullica Twp, NJ late in the morning on June 19. Unseasonably dry, windy conditions were present on the 19th, with relative humidity values near 30 percent and frequent wind gusts of 25 to 35 mph. These conditions, combined with difficulty in accessing the initial fire location, led to rapid fire spread. Following ineffective initial direct attempts at fire control, firefighters conducted backburning operations through June 19-21 to remove fuel ahead of the fire. Combined with gradually improving weather conditions, firefighters gradually achieved full containment of the wildfire. However, by the time it was fully contained, this fire burned an estimated 14,983 acres. This made it the largest wildfire in New Jersey since 2007. No structures were lost during this fire, though several were threatened and protected in nearby campgrounds. No serious injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reference the episode narrative for a summary of events. The start time of the fire is approximated. The end time is the approximate time that near full containment was achieved. However, residual hot spots within the containment zone burned for several days to follow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple clusters of showers and thunderstorms tracked through the mid-Atlantic during the night of June 8 into the early morning of June 9. A warm front was located over the region, with a warm and moist environment present along and south of the front. Moderate wind shear was present, locally enhanced near the frontal boundary. Despite the unfavorable diurnal timing, the shear combined with a small amount of instability allowed for some strong to severe storms developed. These storms produced localized wind damage over New Jersey, along with a brief tornado, as well as heavy rainfall. Storms moved offshore within a couple hours after dawn, and much quieter weather followed behind them.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down onto a garage. Time estimated from radar.

A warm front was located over the region, with a warm and moist environment present along and south of the front. Moderate wind shear was present, locally enhanced near the frontal boundary. Despite the unfavorable diurnal timing, the shear combined with a small amount of instability allowed for some strong to severe storms developed. These storms produced localized wind damage over New Jersey, along with a brief tornado, as well as heavy rainfall. Storms moved offshore within a couple hours after dawn, and much quieter weather followed behind them.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A utility company reported downed and damaged trees in the area. Time estimated from radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down near Erial Road just on the other side |of the Marksmen Landscaping company. The tornado traveled |northeast towards the Deer Park Subdivision where several large |limbs were snapped off of both softwood and hardwood trees, with |at least 3 trees uprooted. In particular, damage began on the|southwest side of Deer Park Circle where several tree limbs were |noted snapped off of hardwood trees just on the other side of the |property line. The tornado then moved northeast, almost parallel |to Deer Park Circle where a hardwood tree was uprooted and fell |on the fence line. Multiple large tree limbs were then snapped at |multiple properties up towards the northeast extent of Deer Park |Circle. Debris was also noted on either side of the road in both |directions. One home had soffit removed from the side facing |northeast. At a single family residence at the end of Deer Park |Circle, two large soft wood trees were uprooted with one tree |having to be removed off of a single family residence. ||The tornado then continued northeast towards high tension |powerlines were it began to quickly dissipate. Several branches |were noted just on the other side of the power lines with PSE&G |Utility trucks removing debris from the clearing. The survey team |then looked on Hidden Drive for any more damage, but only debris |made up of large sticks and leaves was noted. No additional damage|was noted on Hidden Dr or any areas north and east of Hidden Drive |Given that several large trees were uprooted, the estimated peak |wind speed was set at 90 MPH, with the tornado being rated at an |EF-1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rip currents off the Jersey shore resulted in 1 fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fatality occurred on the evening of June 13th near area A22 in Island Beach State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sparked off heavy thunderstorms that slowly moved though northeast New Jersey and the NYC metro area. These thunderstorms dropped between 1.5 and 2.5 inches of rain in less than 2 hours which resulted in areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of Oradell Ave and Elm Street is closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sparked off heavy thunderstorms that slowly moved though northeast New Jersey and the NYC metro area. These thunderstorms dropped between 1.5 and 2.5 inches of rain in less than 2 hours which resulted in areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two underpasses in Allendale NJ are closed due to 2 feet of water. West Crescent Ave and West Orchard Street underpasses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm brought widespread precipitation to the region. A coating of snow and some light wintry mixed precipitation fell across portions of northern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from around 2 inches in the lower elevations to 5 to 7 inches in the higher elevations of Sussex County. The highest snowfall report of 7.2 inches was reported 2 miles SSE of High Point. Some light sleet and freezing rain also mixed in as precipitation ended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm brought widespread precipitation to the region. A coating of snow and some light wintry mixed precipitation fell across portions of northern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations ranged from near 1 inch in the lower elevations to 2 to 4 inches in the higher elevations of Warren County. The highest report was 3.8 inches in Knowlton Twp. Some light sleet and freezing rain occurred as precipitation ended and temperatures warmed above freezing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front swept through the region on December 23rd ushering in a very cold and dry airmass. Temperatures fell into the single digits and teens with wind chills ranging from -5�� to -20�� in New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills ranging from -10�� to -20�� occurred based on area temperature and wind speed observations. The lowest wind chills occurred at the higher elevations of Morris County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front swept through the region with light precipitation lingering for a brief period in its wake. Temperatures plummeted below freezing before paved surfaces dried out. This resulted in icy areas on untreated surfaces.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lingering light precipitation in the wake of a strong Arctic cold front resulted in a flash freeze of untreated surfaces and icy roads as temperatures plummeted well below freezing.
